metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC4 that exercises the failure path, e.g.: `python -c "import subprocess,sys; r=subprocess.run(['python','scripts/gen_cli_reference.py','--inject-bad-cmd'],capture_output=True); sys.exit(0) if r.returncode != 0 else (print('ROBUSTNESS-FAIL') or sys.exit(1))"` — or equivalently, temporarily pass a nonexistent subcommand name and assert the generator exits non-zero (prints CAUGHT-NONZERO).
findings.finding: AC4 (subprocess robustness — exit-code/non-empty check) has no proving command. The requirement says 'a failed/empty --help makes the generator EXIT NON-ZERO (never silently emit a blank entry)' but no verification step exercises this failure path, making the AC unverifiable in-session beyond code inspection.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to AC4: a one-liner that calls the generator with a synthetic bad intercept command (e.g., via an env var or a `--test-bad-cmd` flag) and asserts non-zero exit:
```
python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py --test-bad-cmd NOSUCHCMD 2>/dev/null && echo ROBUSTNESS-FAIL || echo ROBUSTNESS-OK
```
Alternatively, temporarily rename one intercept binary and run the generator, asserting exit code != 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the `enrich` command's lack of a `--help` handler: either add an argparse parser to `cmd_enrich()` (preferred — gives users actual help), or specify that the generator uses a hardcoded inline help string for `enrich` rather than a subprocess capture, and document this exception in the generator's source.
findings.finding: `rebar enrich --help` does not produce help text: `cmd_enrich()` in `rebar/llm/enrich_drain.py` has no argparse parser and will silently attempt a tracker `drain()` call (or fail with a missing-tracker error) when passed `["--help"]`, making the plan's subprocess `--help` capture strategy fail for this command.
findings.location: Plan section 'Source of truth' / Family 2 intercept list / AC 'Subprocess robustness'; rebar/llm/enrich_drain.py lines 303-312
findings.suggested_fix: Add an argparse parser to `cmd_enrich()` in `rebar/llm/enrich_drain.py` (similar to the other intercept commands) so `rebar enrich --help` exits 0 with usage text. Alternatively, note in the plan that `enrich` is documented via a hardcoded docstring in the generator, and add it to an `INLINE_HELP` dict alongside `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS`.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC4's verification to also check `gen - ladder` (the reverse direction) so that entries in `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` that no longer appear in the `argv[0] ==` ladder are also flagged, making the drift check truly bidirectional as the 'Source of truth' section promises.
findings.finding: The 'Source of truth' section says the intercept drift check 'asserts the list still matches' (bidirectional), but AC4's verification code only checks one direction: `missing = ladder - gen` (ladder ⊆ gen_list). Commands present in `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` that were subsequently REMOVED from the `argv[0] == ...` ladder would not be caught — gen_list could become a stale superset of the ladder.
findings.location: Source of truth section ('drift check asserts the list still matches') vs. AC4 verification snippet (`missing=ladder-gen`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second diff to the AC4 snippet: `extra=gen-ladder; print('GEN-NOT-IN-LADDER:', sorted(extra)) if extra else None` — and require the combined check to print nothing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the intercept-list drift check bidirectional: add a second assertion that checks `gen - ladder` is empty (i.e., every name in `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` appears as a literal in the `argv[0] ==` ladder), so phantom/stale generator entries are caught symmetrically.
findings.finding: The intercept-list drift check in AC is asymmetric: it only asserts `ladder ⊆ INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` (a ladder command missing from the generator is caught), but never asserts `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS ⊆ ladder` (a phantom command in the generator list that has no ladder arm is not caught). If someone adds a name to `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` that doesn't correspond to a real `if argv[0] == "..."` arm — or if a ladder arm is renamed and INTERCEPT_COMMANDS is not updated to remove the old name — the set-diff check passes silently, and the generator will invoke `rebar <phantom> --help` as a subprocess. Whether the subprocess check catches this depends on whether `rebar <phantom>` happens to succeed via the `known_subcommands()` path (a family-1 command of the same name would answer `--help` successfully, masking the bug entirely).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The generator's hardcoded intercept list matches...' (drift check AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC's proof script to also compute `gen - ladder` and fail if non-empty: `extra=gen-ladder; print('GEN-NOT-IN-LADDER:', sorted(extra)) if extra else None`. This makes the check a true equality assertion between the two sets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a reverse check (`gen - ladder`) to AC4's verification snippet, or document explicitly that the one-directional check is intentional and that AC5's subprocess failure is the intended backstop for the reverse direction.
findings.finding: AC4's ladder-vs-generator drift check is structurally one-directional (`ladder - gen` only), while the plan prose in the Scope section claims bidirectional coverage ('asserts the list still matches'). A stale INTERCEPT_COMMANDS entry — a command removed from the __init__.py ladder but still in the hardcoded list — passes the AC4 check silently. AC5's subprocess robustness check may catch this at generation time (the removed command's `--help` likely fails), but this is incidental rather than specified.
findings.location: AC4 verification snippet / Scope section ('a drift check asserts the list still matches the if argv[0] == "..." intercept ladder')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC4 one-liner to also compute `extra = gen - ladder; print('GEN-NOT-IN-LADDER:', sorted(extra)) if extra else None` so both directions are explicit. Alternatively, add a sentence to AC4 clarifying that the reverse direction (gen ⊄ ladder) is intentionally caught by AC5's subprocess exit-code check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` and `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` (both `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` entry and the scripts regex coverage) to the Files section and scope, mirroring the complete env-registry gate pattern that existing parity-enforcement machinery enforces.
findings.finding: The plan partially copies the env-registry drift-gate pattern into test.yml but omits the two co-required consumers that the repo's own enforcement machinery will immediately trip on: (1) gerrit-verify.yaml must receive the same step (test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci auto-catches any scripts/*.py in test.yml missing from gerrit-verify.yaml and fails `make test`); (2) _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES in tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py must be updated with the new gate's signature. The plan's Files section lists only test.yml, so an implementer following the plan exactly will produce a broken suite.
findings.location: Scope ('Wire a CI drift gate in .github/workflows/test.yml mirroring the env-registry gate') and Files section (lists only test.yml)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Files: `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` (add the gen_cli_reference.py drift-gate step, mirroring lines 208-222 of the existing file). Add to Files: `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` (add `"cli-reference drift gate": "scripts/gen_cli_reference.py"` to `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES`). This makes the plan's own AC ('make test stays green') achievable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` to the Files list and Scope, wiring `gen_cli_reference.py` into that workflow the same way `gen_env_registry.py` is wired, so `test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci` stays green.
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py enforces (via test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci) that every scripts/*.py gate wired into test.yml must also appear in gerrit-verify.yaml. Wiring gen_cli_reference.py only into test.yml (as the plan specifies in Scope and Files) will cause this parity test to fail immediately. gerrit-verify.yaml is an unenumerated consumer that must be updated alongside test.yml.
findings.location: Scope section ('Wire a CI drift gate in .github/workflows/test.yml'); Files section (lists test.yml but omits gerrit-verify.yaml)
findings.suggested_fix: Add gerrit-verify.yaml to the Files section and mirror the env-registry drift gate block (python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py + git diff --exit-code -- docs/cli-reference.md) in gerrit-verify.yaml's build-and-test job, exactly as gen_env_registry.py is mirrored at line 215 of that file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` to the Files list and note that a new `'cli-reference drift gate': 'scripts/gen_cli_reference.py'` entry must be added to `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES`.
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py also maintains _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES, which maps each named drift gate to a stable command signature that must appear in BOTH test.yml and gerrit-verify.yaml. Adding the CLI reference drift gate without adding a corresponding entry to _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES means the gate is not tracked by name in the parity test (it would still be caught by the script-regex test, but a later rename could silently drop it from _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES coverage). This test file is an unenumerated consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add subprocess robustness (exit-code check + non-empty output guard → generator exits non-zero) to the Scope section's description of the family-2 capture step, so the requirement appears in both the body and the AC.
findings.finding: AC5 (subprocess robustness: failed/empty `rebar <cmd> --help` makes the generator exit non-zero) has no corresponding description in the Scope/body. The Scope describes family-2 capture only as 'family 2 via the explicit intercept list + `rebar <cmd> --help`' with no mention of error handling, making AC5 an orphan criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for family 2, append: 'each subprocess call MUST verify exit code == 0 and non-empty stdout; any failure aborts the generator with a non-zero exit code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope description of gen_cli_reference.py: (a) command names must be rendered backtick-wrapped in the output, and (b) the hardcoded intercept list must be exported as the module-level constant `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS`.
findings.finding: Two specific implementation requirements — (a) the generated file must use backtick-wrapped command names (AC2) and (b) the generator must expose its intercept list as the module-level constant `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` (AC4) — appear only in the ACs, with no mention in the Scope/body description of the generator, creating orphan criteria.
findings.location: Scope section vs. AC2 (backtick wrapping) and AC4 (INTERCEPT_COMMANDS export)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief CLAUDE.md (or equivalent developer-facing doc) update capturing: (1) the two-generator-script CI gate pattern, (2) the rule that adding a new intercept arm in `_cli/__init__.py` requires updating `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` in `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py`, and (3) how to regenerate `docs/cli-reference.md` locally.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new developer-maintenance contract — 'when you add a new CLI intercept arm in _cli/__init__.py, you must also update INTERCEPT_COMMANDS in scripts/gen_cli_reference.py' — but does not update CLAUDE.md or any design doc to capture this workflow. The CI drift gate will catch violations after the fact, but no forward guidance exists for contributors.
findings.location: Scope section / Files: scripts/gen_cli_reference.py (new) / Acceptance Criteria — INTERCEPT_COMMANDS drift check AC
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, add `CLAUDE.md` and include a Scope bullet: 'Update CLAUDE.md with a "CLI reference maintenance" section explaining the INTERCEPT_COMMANDS contract and the `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` regeneration workflow.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-subprocess timeout (e.g., `timeout=10` seconds) to each `rebar <cmd> --help` call and surface a clear error (command name + 'timed out') before exiting non-zero, so a hanging help invocation fails fast.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a failed or empty `rebar <cmd> --help` subprocess exits non-zero, but specifies no timeout for subprocess calls. A command that hangs (e.g., waits on stdin, blocks on a lock, or hits a bug) would stall CI indefinitely across all 16 intercept-arm captures.
findings.suggested_fix: In `gen_cli_reference.py`, use `subprocess.run([..., cmd, '--help'], capture_output=True, timeout=10, ...)` and catch `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` to print `ERROR: 'rebar {cmd} --help' timed out` and exit non-zero.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Scope section explaining why subprocess capture (rather than in-process argparse parser import) is the right approach for family-2 intercept commands — e.g., whether it's because the commands have side-effectful initialization before `--help` is parsed, or because subprocess faithfully captures the exact runtime help string.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing the subprocess-capture approach for family-2 intercept commands over in-process argparse introspection. Each intercept command already constructs an `argparse.ArgumentParser` internally (confirmed in `_llm_commands.py`); a generator could import the module and call the parser directly without spawning subprocesses, avoiding any CI environment/config dependency. The absence of a stated rationale leaves implementers without guidance on this non-obvious design decision and makes it harder to evaluate whether the subprocess approach is correct for all 16 intercepts.
findings.location: Scope section — 'family 2 via the explicit intercept list + rebar <cmd> --help'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the generator should verify `rebar` is importable/executable before invoking subprocesses (e.g. pre-flight `rebar --help` check or `python -m rebar ...` invocation) and emit a clear error distinguishing 'rebar not installed' from 'command --help failed'.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `rebar <cmd> --help` is invoked via subprocess for all 16 intercept commands, but does not define what the generator should do when the `rebar` entry point is not installed or not on PATH in the CI environment where the drift gate runs — the generator could fail for environment reasons unrelated to actual command staleness.
findings.location: Scope section ('captures each via `rebar <cmd> --help` (subprocess)') / AC5 ('each `rebar <cmd> --help` capture checks the exit code and non-empty output')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-flight step: `subprocess.run(['rebar', '--help'], check=True)` or use `python -m rebar` to ensure the subprocess resolves through the same Python environment. Document this in AC5.

findings.finding: AC4 ('the intercept list in the generator matches the _cli/__init__.py intercept ladder') has no proving command that actually exercises or verifies the drift check fires when a new intercept arm is added. The AC only states the check 'is' present, making it non-measurably stated and unverifiable without reading the source.

The completeness-proof commands in AC2 use `grep -qF "$c"` (fixed-string, substring match), which produces a false-positive for short command names that appear as substrings of longer ones. For example, `grep -qF 'review' docs/cli-reference.md` will succeed if only 'review-code' or 'review-plan' is present, silently masking the absence of the standalone 'review' command. Similarly, 'config' or 'prompt' could match incidentally in prose or other command output text.
findings.suggested_fix: Change both `grep -qF "$c"` calls to `grep -qE "(^|[^a-z-])${c}([^a-z-]|$)"` or, better, rely on a structural anchor matching the generated markdown heading pattern, e.g., `grep -qF "## ${c}"` (or whatever heading prefix the generator uses), so that 'review' only matches the '## review' section heading.

findings.finding: The `.github/workflows/test.yml` modification is listed as a named deliverable in both Scope and Files, but no AC verifies that the CI gate was actually wired in the workflow file. AC3 only validates the `--check` flag locally; an implementer could satisfy all five ACs without touching `.github/workflows/test.yml`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all 5 items); Scope section ('Wire a CI drift gate in `.github/workflows/test.yml`'); Files: `.github/workflows/test.yml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '[ ] `.github/workflows/test.yml` contains a step that runs `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py --check` (or equivalent `git diff --exit-code` gate): `grep -q gen_cli_reference .github/workflows/test.yml && echo OK`'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
